# Questions in Livestream 160

Live Stream #160: The Synchronicity Of 13,000 Subs

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=453" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:33</a> | Will modern English translations age the way the King James Version did? | He says yes, because language shifts over time and even comparatively recent translations already feel dated. | He points to the American Standard Version as already sounding archaic, not mainly because scholarship stopped improving but because English itself changes quickly. He adds that translation committees such as the NIV's keep revising partly because diction and usage can shift even within one lifetime. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=845" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:05</a> | Are there proto-mummies that show how Egyptian mummification developed, or why Egyptians started the process? | He says mummification first happened naturally in dry sand burials, and intentional mummification tried to recreate that natural preservation. | He rejects the idea of a separate proto-mummy stage and instead describes poor sand-pit burials where dry sand preserved bodies extremely well. By the Old Kingdom, intentional mummification was trying to reproduce what those simple pit graves had already achieved naturally. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=995" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:35</a> | Did Moses know before fleeing Egypt that his family line would become future rulers? | He says the text does not tell us, though Moses may have known he had been adopted into Egypt's highest governing circles. | He says the biblical text does not answer the question directly. As a historical reconstruction, Moses may have known his adopted family was rising because his adopted grandfather was already a vizier and had been named crown prince, but the dynasty's future was not yet official until later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=1109" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:29</a> | What is his favorite part of Cecil B. DeMille's Ten Commandments? | He says the Egyptian dancing scene, because the dance movements are unusually historically accurate. | He singles out the Egyptian dance scene with the circle of women as unusually accurate, saying the stretched-leg movements match poses known from tomb paintings. He says he has not seen another Exodus film recreate Egyptian dancing as well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=1239" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:39</a> | Does Isaiah 3:12 settle egalitarian debates? | He says the passage is not a simple modern proof text and needs to be read in its own context. | He reads Isaiah 3:12 as an ancient Near Eastern inversion text, where normal social order is pictured as turned upside down to show chaos and failed leadership. On that reading, the point is not a direct comment on modern egalitarianism but a literary image of disorder, with leaders failing to lead properly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=1543" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:43</a> | Did he watch the Gavin Ortlund and Trent Horn dialogue with Kip and Bowen, and what were his thoughts? | He says the setup was skewed by the moderator, the central slavery objection was never really argued, and Ortlund and Horn were too trusting about how the exchange would be reframed later. | He says the framing of two apologists against two critical scholars prejudiced the audience from the start and treated faith and scholarship as opposites. His main criticism is that the claim "the Bible endorses slavery" was not actually pressed, leaving a meandering exchange that he expects opponents to reframe afterward. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=2094" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:54</a> | Did he notice semantic games, tricks, or logical fallacies in that exchange? | He says there were semantic games around chattel slavery and a straw man about U.S. slavery, but not much coherent logic or exegesis to analyze. | He says there were definitional games around "chattel slavery," but not much sustained logic because the debate did little real exegesis. He gives as an example a later misrepresentation that Ortlund had minimized U.S. slavery, when Fulk says the actual claim concerned biblical slavery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=2315" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:35</a> | Does the Bible endorse slavery? | He says no; he frames the biblical laws as regulation of an existing ancient institution rather than endorsement of it. | He compares slavery laws to divorce laws: regulation of an existing practice does not equal approval. He argues ancient societies lacked modern debt, banking, courts, and prison systems, so debt slavery functioned within a different economic structure that Scripture regulates without presenting as morally ideal. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=2722" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:22</a> | What grade would Douglas Petrovich's dissertation have received? | He says he would have failed it because he considered it below standard. | In the surrounding discussion of British PhD outcomes, he distinguishes a pass with minor corrections from major corrections, an MPhil-level outcome, and a fail. When asked specifically about Petrovich's dissertation, he says he would have failed or "MPhil'd" it because he thought it was not up to standard. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=2765" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:05</a> | Are sacraments a means of salvation? | He says sacraments are important acts of faith and obedience, but Christ is the means of salvation, not the sacraments themselves. | He distinguishes his view from a Lutheran-style answer that baptism gives saving grace. He defines sacraments as church ordinances such as baptism and the Lord's Supper, but says they instantiate and strengthen faith rather than saving in themselves; salvation belongs to Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=2975" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:35</a> | What does baptism do? | He presents baptism as initiation and a first act of repentance and obedience after committing oneself to Christ. | He describes baptism as public initiation into belonging to Christ: the first public act of repentance, obedience, and recognition after one's commitment. It does not save by itself, but it belongs to a broader path of increasing faithfulness and obedience. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=3151" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:31</a> | What would he say to those who call baptism a purely symbolic act? | He says it is not merely symbolic, but a necessary act predicated on faith and a next step in that faith. | He rejects calling baptism "purely symbolic" because he sees it as a necessary act grounded in faith. His phrasing keeps it from being an automatic saving mechanism, but still treats it as a real act of obedience and a next step in growing faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=3299" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:59</a> | When did fundamentalism take root in America? | He places its rise in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries as a reactionary movement. | He traces it to a reaction against European higher criticism, especially the documentary hypothesis, and to defending perceived fundamentals such as the virgin birth, resurrection, grace through faith, the second coming, and the Trinity. He also distinguishes later fundamentalism from evangelicalism by second-degree separation, pre-tribulational rapture commitments, and a practical wooden literalism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=3811" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:31</a> | What was the Witch of Endor expecting in 1 Samuel 28? | He says she likely expected ordinary mediumistic activity, not Samuel himself, and her shock shows something unexpected happened. | He reads the woman's fear and surprise as evidence that she expected a familiar spirit she normally worked with, not the real Samuel. The text's presentation of Samuel appearing and rebuking Saul is, in his view, precisely what shocks her. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=4103" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:23</a> | How should familiar spirits be understood? | He identifies them as demonic or fallen spiritual powers rather than harmless human spirits. | He identifies familiar spirits with demons, fallen powers, or unclean spirits that can deceive and pretend to be the dead. He treats modern claims of familiar spirits as the same basic activity with different people involved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=4390" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:10</a> | Why did Moses go far from Midian to pasture his flocks? | He says the Sinai region had grasslands and Moses was following practical grazing conditions. | He explains the move as a resource issue: Midian was mostly desert and had limited pasture, while Sinai still had grasslands and watering places. Moses, like other pastoral groups, went farther out because that is where flocks could actually graze. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=4476" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:36</a> | Has he heard anyone speak an "angelic tongue" that sounded like a real language? | He says no, despite hearing such claims. | He says he has heard claims of angelic tongues, but not one that sounded like a genuine language. To illustrate, he reads a passage in an unfamiliar real language and says even listeners who cannot identify it can hear features such as cadence and phonology that mark it as language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=5120" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:20</a> | Were mummified rabbits ever a thing in ancient Egypt? | He says yes, though they are not as common as some other animal mummies. | He says rabbit mummies are known but rare, especially compared with falcon, ibis, or cat mummies. He connects them particularly with worshippers of the hare goddess Wenet and notes that examples could be wrapped, painted, and decorated similarly to cat mummies, including preserved long ears. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=5222" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:02</a> | Were the apostles likely teenagers? | He says yes; first-century disciples were often young men, commonly around ages 15 to 19, though some could be older or younger. | He compares discipleship to a lower-cost religious education path for young men who could not enter elite schools. In his reconstruction, most disciples would have been in their late teens, with some variation; even Peter could have been about nineteen or twenty and already married because marriage depended heavily on trade and economic status. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=5570" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:50</a> | What are his favorite ancient Egyptian graffiti examples? | He points to graffiti on abandoned temples, including Coptic marks and Egyptian humorous or crude graffiti, and says Egyptian graffiti was often funnier than Roman graffiti. | He says abandoned temples often accumulated graffiti, such as Coptic crosses, writing, animal drawings, and even a crucified figure he recalls seeing. He contrasts Egyptian graffiti favorably with Roman graffiti, saying Egyptian examples could include humorous scenes like animals serving other animals drink rather than only vulgarity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=5872" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:52</a> | What does "raised for our justification" mean in Romans 4:25? | He reads it as showing the completed work of Christ, contrasting being delivered up for transgressions with being raised for justification. | He reads Romans 4:25 as a contrastive verse: delivered up versus raised, and transgressions versus justification. The resurrection shows the completion of Christ's redemptive work, not merely atonement for sin but the defeat of sin and death so that justification results from the finished work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=6100" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:40</a> | What criteria are used to number Egyptian dynasties and prevent them from being treated as co-regencies? | He says dynasties are judged by succession, blood relation, art or architectural change, kingdom domain, and other evidence; the sixth and thirteenth dynasties cannot be made parallel. | He explains that dynasties can be grouped by blood succession, but also by practical markers such as architecture, art style, and political domain, especially in Manetho. For the sixth and thirteenth dynasties, he says monuments, administration, capitals, king lists, and the Old Kingdom/Middle Kingdom separation rule out treating them as parallel dynasties. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=6580" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:40</a> | What does "like a dove" mean in Luke 3:22? | He says it is a simile for a visible bodily descent, and also evokes ancient Near Eastern bird imagery associated with kingship. | He says the phrase describes the Spirit descending in visible bodily form the way a bird settles on a person. He then connects it with ancient Near Eastern royal imagery, such as the Horus bird settling on the Egyptian king, so the scene signals Jesus' kingship as well as the visible descent of the Spirit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=7100" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:20</a> | Who was the worst or most evil pharaoh? | He distinguishes incompetence from evil, naming Ptolemy XIII as very incompetent and pointing to Persian pharaohs as especially brutal. | He separates "worst" from "most evil": Ptolemy XIII is named as exceptionally incompetent, while Persian rulers such as Artaxerxes III or Darius II are treated as better candidates for evil. His reason is the Persian record of brutal repression in Egypt, including temple plundering, massacres, and impaling. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=7446" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:06</a> | Did ancient Egyptians have an all-encompassing concept of God or fate above lesser gods, like the One in Neoplatonism? | He says no; Egyptian usage of "god" was usually contextual, with Atenist and later Amun-Re manifestation language being the closest parallels. | He says Egyptian references to "God" or "the god" normally depend on the local context and may refer to whichever deity is in view, not to a philosophical supreme One. The closest parallels are Atenist claims that gods were manifestations of Aten and later language applying manifestation ideas to Amun-Re, but he says this still falls short of a true all-encompassing concept. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=7641" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:21</a> | Has his work ever made him question his faith? | He says no; his work has strengthened his faith, though he questioned his faith more as a teenager before entering that work. | He says academic work did not dry out or undermine his faith because he approached even technical study as worship. The more he studied Scripture and the ancient Near East, the more connections he saw, such as the Luke 3 and Isaiah 3 examples from the same stream, and those discoveries strengthened rather than weakened his faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=8011" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:31</a> | What was the most decisive factor for victory in ancient Near Eastern warfare? | He answers logistics, emphasizing supply and practical organization. | He says tactics may win battles, but logistics wins wars: armies must be fed, supplied, mobile, and organized. He illustrates this with Alexander's smaller but fast and well-supplied army, Roman logistical systems, and even shepherds following early Mesopotamian armies to manage captured flocks. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=8245" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:25</a> | What rights did ancient Near Eastern women have, such as property or autonomy? | He says rights varied by culture, but Egyptian women had broad rights to work, earn income, own property, adopt, and enter or leave marriages. | He limits the answer mainly to Egypt and says Egyptian women had more rights than many other ancient Near Eastern women. They could work, earn income, own and pass on property, hold jobs, adopt children, liberate slaves, and enter or leave marriages on their own volition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=8399" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:59</a> | What academic study Bible would he recommend for the Old and New Testament? | He says there is not one best option for both testaments; he generally likes CSB and ESV study Bibles, especially archaeology-focused ones. | He says the best option depends on what kind of study Bible is needed, such as archaeology or historical theology. He generally praises CSB and ESV study Bibles, mentions the CSB Ancient Faith Bible and ESV archaeology materials, and is more critical of many New King James and NASB study Bible offerings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=8678" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:38</a> | Has he changed his mind that eating Christ's flesh and drinking his blood in John 6 is not about communion? | He says no; he still reads that passage as about commitment to Christ rather than directly about communion. | He says Christ is still alive in the passage and no direct connection to communion is made there. He reads the language as a call to total commitment, parallel to picking up one's cross and following Christ, with Passover resonances but not a direct communion reference. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=8842" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:22</a> | What little-known disturbing ancient Egypt facts does he like to tell people? | He gives examples involving mummification, including the smell and the way wet mummies can collapse into a mass of remains. | He first mentions a disturbing contraceptive reference, then focuses on what happens when mummies get wet. In examples such as flooded museum storage or waterlogged sarcophagi, he says flesh can dissolve, leaving rags, bones, sludge, and foul-smelling water rather than an intact mummy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=9327" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:27</a> | What happens to those who have never heard the gospel or of Jesus Christ? | He says the issue is controversial, but argues they may be given a real encounter with Christ, while not being automatically saved or excluded. | He says Christians disagree on this and the Bible does not spell out every detail. His preferred possibility is that those who never encountered Christ in life may be presented with him and genuinely choose or reject him, so ignorance is neither an automatic pass nor an automatic exclusion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=9601" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:01</a> | How did ancient Egyptians start fires? | He says they used both bow drills and flint, with bow drills more likely earlier and flint-and-steel methods later. | He says Egypt had abundant flint and that Egyptians used both flint and bow drills. Because steel was not common in the Old Kingdom, he thinks bow drills would have been more likely earlier, while by the Third Intermediate Period flint-and-steel methods were more common. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=9688" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:28</a> | Is there evidence Hazor was rebuilt immediately after Joshua's destruction? | He says no; he reads Judges as referring to a king of Hazor rather than requiring a rebuilt city, using a transfer-of-flag idea. | He says the 1210 BC destruction was Hazor's final destruction and Judges does not require the city itself to have been rebuilt. Instead, Hazor was a confederacy, so when the capital was destroyed the political "flag" could transfer to another city while Jabin retained the title king of Hazor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=9954" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:54</a> | Is the Hazor transfer-of-flag idea like the Portuguese monarchy relocating to Brazil after Napoleon conquered Portugal? | He says yes, comparing both to an administrative or political identity moving to a different place. | He accepts the comparison because the ruling identity can continue after the old capital is lost. He also gives an ancient example, saying Pi-Ramesses or the capital of the Tjeku province could move administratively to a new site while retaining the older political name. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=10037" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:17</a> | How can someone set up a podcast with him? | He says to contact him through Facebook or similar messaging, then he checks whether the conversation would be a good fit. | He says Facebook Messenger is his usual route for arranging interviews, with Twitter as a secondary possibility and email discouraged. After contact, he checks the show and usually says yes if there is a good possibility of a real conversation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=10172" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:32</a> | Could some cryptids be possessed animals that mimic human voices or behave strangely? | He says it is possible in principle because Scripture has animals being possessed, but it is hard to prove and could also be ordinary animal behavior. | After joking through several examples, he answers seriously that possession of animals is possible in principle because of biblical examples such as unclean spirits entering pigs. He immediately qualifies that this would be difficult to prove and that supposed cases could just be wild animals behaving like animals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=10586" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:26</a> | If he lived in ancient Egypt, what would his occupation be? | He says he would probably be a metalsmith, because he can make knives and jewelry and could fit into that craft class. | He says he would not imagine himself in a political role but as a craftsman. Because he can read, communicate, make knives, make jewelry, and work metal, he thinks he would fit well into the metalsmith craft class. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=10835" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:35</a> | Has he seen Mike Winger's video on Benny Hinn, and what is his view of Hinn? | He says he has many opinions and describes Hinn as a long-standing false prophet and con man. | He says Hinn's problems have been public since the 1990s, so recent surprise over Winger's video frustrates him. His critique focuses on prosperity and healing claims that burden sick people by implying illness persists because they lack faith, along with repeated public repentances followed by the same behavior. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=11613" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:13:33</a> | Are there televangelists he thinks are genuine rather than grifters or heretics? | He says none come to mind, arguing that expensive television ministry tends to create pressure toward fundraising abuse. | He says no examples come to mind because television ministry is extremely expensive compared with radio or smaller platforms. In his view, as a ministry's costs and staff grow, the pressure to fundraise can turn into increasing grift, while shepherds fail to protect the flock from exploitative figures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=11900" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:20</a> | What ancient technologies would surprise people today? | He rejects weaponized mirrors but points to automata, mechanical birds, holy-water vending machines, and moving statues. | He dismisses weaponized mirrors as History Channel-style material, but says ancient automata would surprise modern people. His examples include flapping mechanical birds, temple vending machines that dispensed holy water after a coin was inserted, and statues that moved or posed at certain times. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=12067" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:07</a> | Did Canaanites practice cannibalism? | He says there is slight evidence for one early Bronze Age context involving eating the flesh of the dead before burying bones under houses, but not broad evidence beyond that. | He says the evidence is limited to one early Bronze Age context connected with in-house burials, where the dead may have been defleshed or consumed before bones were buried under the floor. He stresses that this is slight evidence and not proof of a broad or later Canaanite practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=12168" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:48</a> | Why is the New Revised Standard Version a default choice in academia? | He says it is often treated as the least religious translation, with a good critical base text and a more secular translation tendency. | He says the NRSV has a good critical textual base, including standard Greek and Hebrew editions, but its translation choices tend toward the less religious option when choices arise. That secular tendency, in his view, explains why it often becomes the default in academic contexts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=12277" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:37</a> | Is Kenneth Copeland a cryptid? | He treats it as a joke, then criticizes Copeland's wealth, estate, and private planes as inappropriate for a minister. | He jokes about Copeland's camera presence, then shifts to criticism of ministry wealth, including reports of multiple private planes and a large estate. He says private security can be understandable for widely known ministers, but ministry-funded excess and private aviation on that scale are not becoming of a minister of the gospel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=12965" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:05</a> | Does Leviticus 25:44-46 apply only at Jubilee or always, and could foreign slaves be bought at any time? | He says foreign slaves were an exception to the Jubilee release rules, could be bought at any time, and the broader slavery questions remain debated. | He reads the passage as contrasting Israelite debt slaves, who could be released in the seventh year or at Jubilee, with foreign slaves who were not released by those rules. He adds that foreign slaves could be bought at any time, while acknowledging that the broader lines and applications of slavery law are debated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=13368" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:42:48</a> | What was the Lord thinking when he created the monkfish and called it good? | He jokes that the fish is ugly but very tasty, comparing it to lobster in flavor and texture. | He answers the joke by saying monkfish is good because it tastes good, not because it looks good. He describes it as "poor man's lobster," with a lobster-like texture and flavor, while agreeing that its appearance leaves much to be desired. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=13643" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:23</a> | When might his fiction series be available? | He says he has no estimate; two of three books are done, he still needs to finish the third and find a literary agent. | He says there is no estimate yet because two novels are complete, the third still needs to be finished, and he has not started the literary-agent process. He describes the project only broadly as science fiction involving ancient spiritual powers, not UFO material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=13912" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:52</a> | Which is better for the city of Terah: Kesh or Urfa? | He strongly favors Kesh, citing linguistic, chronological, and biographical problems with the Urfa view and a close match between Terah and a known figure from Kesh. | He argues Kesh fits better because Urfa is Hittite-speaking and north of Haran, while Kesh fits the Amorite linguistic context and closely resembles "Kashdim." He also says a known Terah-like figure from Kesh has a strikingly similar biography, including a son dying there, leaving after political strife, becoming a sojourner or Habiru, and moving into Syria. |
